question: What is a closure? Give a real use.
answer:
A closure is a function that keeps access to variables from its outer (lexical) scope even after the outer function has returned. The inner function "remembers" the environment it was created in.

Uses: data privacy (private variables), function factories, and holding state in callbacks — e.g. a debounce keeps its timer id in a closure.
tip: Closures are the answer to 'how do you make a private variable in JavaScript'.
deep:
The definition, precisely

A closure is the combination of a function and the lexical environment it was defined in. When you create a function inside another function, the inner one keeps a live reference to the outer one's variables — even after the outer function has returned and its call is gone from the stack.

Why it works

Scope in JS is lexical (decided by where code is written, not how it's called). The inner function's scope chain still points at the outer variables, so the garbage collector keeps them alive as long as the closure exists.

function makeCounter() {
  let n = 0;              // private, lives on
  return {
    inc() { return ++n; },
    reset() { n = 0; }
  };
}
const c = makeCounter();
c.inc(); // 1
c.inc(); // 2   — n is shared & hidden

Real-world uses

  • Data privacy / encapsulation — n above can't be touched from outside.
  • Function factories — const add5 = makeAdder(5).
  • Stateful callbacks — debounce/throttle keep their timer id in a closure.
  • Memoization — cache stored in the enclosing scope.
  • Event handlers — remember data from when they were attached.
function makeAdder(x) { return y => x + y; }
const add5 = makeAdder(5);
add5(2); // 7

The classic gotcha (loop + var)

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0); // 3, 3, 3
}
// fix: use let (new binding per iteration) → 0, 1, 2

All three callbacks close over the same var i; by the time they run, the loop finished and i is 3. let creates a fresh binding each iteration.

How to say it in an interview

“A closure is a function that remembers the variables from the scope where it was created, even after that scope has exited. It’s how JavaScript does private state — I use it for factories, memoization, and keeping state like a debounce timer.”

question: var vs let vs const, and what is hoisting?
answer:
• var — function-scoped, hoisted and initialised to undefined, re-declarable.
  • let — block-scoped, hoisted but in the Temporal Dead Zone (error if used before declaration).
  • const — block-scoped, assigned once. The binding is constant, not the object — you can still mutate a const array/object.

Hoisting: declarations are moved to the top of their scope at compile time. var and function declarations are usable early; let/const are not.
tip: Default to const, use let when you must reassign, avoid var.
deep:
Side-by-side

varletconst
Scopefunctionblockblock
Hoistedyes → undefinedyes → TDZyes → TDZ
Re-declareallowednono
Re-assignyesyesno
Global propadds to windownono

What hoisting actually is

At compile time the engine scans a scope and registers its declarations before running any line. So the declaration is “moved up,” but the assignment stays put.

console.log(a); // undefined (declaration hoisted, value not)
var a = 5;

console.log(b); // ReferenceError (TDZ)
let b = 5;

var is initialised to undefined when hoisted; let/const are hoisted but left uninitialised — reading them before the declaration line throws (the Temporal Dead Zone).

Block scope matters

if (true) { var x = 1; let y = 2; }
console.log(x); // 1  — var leaks out of the block
console.log(y); // ReferenceError — let is block-scoped

const is not "immutable"

const freezes the binding, not the value. You can still mutate the contents of a const object/array — use Object.freeze() for shallow immutability.

const arr = [1];
arr.push(2);   // ✅ allowed — same reference
arr = [];      // ❌ TypeError — reassigning the binding

Function declarations vs expressions

Function declarations are fully hoisted (callable before their line). Function expressions assigned to var/let follow the variable's hoisting rules.

How to say it in an interview

“Use const by default, let when you must reassign, and avoid var because it’s function-scoped and leaks. Hoisting means declarations are processed before execution — var becomes undefined, while let/const sit in the TDZ and throw if used early.”

question: Explain the event loop, microtasks vs macrotasks.
answer:
JS is single-threaded. The call stack runs synchronous code. Async callbacks wait in queues and the event loop pushes them onto the stack when it's empty.

  • Microtask queue — Promise .then, queueMicrotask, await continuations. Fully drained after each task.
  • Macrotask queue — setTimeout, setInterval, I/O, UI events.

Microtasks always run before the next macrotask.
tip: Classic trick question: a resolved Promise's .then logs before a setTimeout(…, 0).
deep:
The pieces

  • Call stack — where synchronous code runs, one frame at a time.
  • Web/Node APIs — timers, fetch, DOM events run outside the engine and hand back callbacks when done.
  • Macrotask (task) queue — setTimeout, setInterval, I/O, UI events.
  • Microtask queue — Promise .then/.catch/.finally, await continuations, queueMicrotask.

The golden rule

After each macrotask (and after the initial synchronous run), the engine drains the entire microtask queue before rendering or picking up the next macrotask. Microtasks always beat macrotasks.

console.log('A');
setTimeout(() => console.log('B'), 0);   // macrotask
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('C')); // microtask
console.log('D');
// A, D, C, B

Order: sync A, D run first; the stack empties; microtask C drains before the timer; macrotask B last.

Microtasks can starve the queue

Promise.resolve().then(function loop() {
  Promise.resolve().then(loop); // keeps adding microtasks
});
setTimeout(() => console.log('never soon'), 0);

Because the microtask queue must fully drain first, an endless chain of microtasks blocks timers and rendering.

await is just Promise scheduling

async function f() {
  console.log(1);
  await null;          // pause; rest becomes a microtask
  console.log(3);
}
console.log(0); f(); console.log(2);
// 0, 1, 2, 3

Everything after an await is scheduled as a microtask — the synchronous code around the call runs first.

How to say it in an interview

“JS runs on one thread with a call stack. Async callbacks wait in queues; the event loop moves them onto the stack when it’s empty. Microtasks — Promise callbacks and await continuations — are fully drained after each task, so a resolved Promise’s .then always runs before a setTimeout(0).”

question: == vs === ?
answer:
== compares after type coercion (0 == "0" is true). === checks value and type with no coercion.

Always use === unless you deliberately want coercion (e.g. x == null to catch both null and undefined).
tip: x == null is the one idiomatic use of == — it matches null and undefined.
code:
0 == '';    // true
0 === '';   // false
null == undefined; // true

question: How does the this keyword work?
answer:
this depends on how a function is called:

  • Regular call → global object, or undefined in strict mode.
  • Method call obj.fn() → obj.
  • Arrow function → no own this; inherits from the enclosing scope.
  • call / apply / bind → set this explicitly.
tip: Arrow functions are why callbacks 'just work' inside classes — they keep the outer this.
deep:
The one rule: it's decided at call time

For a normal function, this is not set by where it's defined — it's set by how it's called. Work through these binding rules in order:

Call formthis is
new Fn()the brand-new object
fn.call/apply/bind(o)o (explicit)
obj.fn()obj (the receiver)
fn() aloneundefined (strict) / global (sloppy)
arrow functioninherited from enclosing scope

The "lost this" bug

const obj = {
  name: 'A',
  greet() { return this.name; }
};
const g = obj.greet;
g();          // undefined — called plain, no receiver
obj.greet();  // 'A'

Detaching a method drops its receiver. Fixes: obj.greet.bind(obj), wrap in an arrow, or define the method as a class field arrow.

Why arrow functions fix callbacks

Arrows have no own this — they capture it lexically from where they're written. That's why a setTimeout or array callback inside a method keeps the right this:

class Timer {
  seconds = 0;
  start() { setInterval(() => this.seconds++, 1000); } // arrow keeps `this`
}

A normal function() { this.seconds++ } here would break — its this would be the timer/global, not the instance.

Don't use arrows for object methods

const o = { v: 1, get() => this.v };
o.get(); // undefined — arrow's this is the outer scope, not o

How to say it in an interview

“this depends on how a function is called, not where it’s defined: new → the new object, call/apply/bind → explicit, obj.fn() → the object, a bare call → undefined in strict mode. Arrow functions have no own this; they inherit it — which is exactly why they’re great for callbacks and wrong for object methods.”

question: Promise vs async/await, and how do you handle errors?
answer:
A Promise represents a future value (pending → fulfilled/rejected). async/await is syntactic sugar that lets asynchronous code read synchronously.

Errors: .catch() on a chain, or try/catch around await. Use Promise.all for parallel and Promise.allSettled when you need every result regardless of failures.
tip: await inside a loop is sequential — use Promise.all to parallelise independent calls.
deep:
A Promise's three states

pending → then either fulfilled (has a value) or rejected (has a reason). It settles once and never changes again.

async/await is sugar over Promises

An async function always returns a Promise. await pauses the function until the awaited Promise settles, unwrapping the value (or throwing the rejection). It reads top-to-bottom but is still non-blocking.

// chain
fetch('/user').then(r => r.json()).then(use).catch(handle);
// same thing, flatter
async function load() {
  try { const r = await fetch('/user'); use(await r.json()); }
  catch (e) { handle(e); }
}

Error handling — the four traps

  • try/catch around await, or .catch() on a chain.
  • fetch doesn't reject on HTTP errors — a 404/500 still fulfils. Check res.ok yourself.
  • A missing catch → an unhandled promise rejection.
  • Forgetting await → you handle the Promise object, not the value, and the try/catch won't catch its rejection.
const res = await fetch(url);
if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${res.status}`);

Sequential vs parallel

// ❌ sequential — waits ~2x
const a = await getA();
const b = await getB();

// ✅ parallel — independent calls at once
const [a, b] = await Promise.all([getA(), getB()]);

await inside a for loop runs one at a time. For independent work, kick them all off and Promise.all — or allSettled when you need every result even if some fail.

How to say it in an interview

“A Promise is a future value; async/await is syntax sugar that makes it read synchronously. I handle errors with try/catch around await — remembering fetch doesn’t reject on 4xx/5xx so I check res.ok — and I use Promise.all to parallelise independent calls instead of awaiting them in sequence.”

question: Debounce vs throttle — what and when?
answer:
Debounce — wait until events stop for N ms, then fire once. Use for search-as-you-type.

Throttle — fire at most once per N ms while events keep coming. Use for scroll/resize/mousemove.
tip: Debounce = 'wait for quiet'; throttle = 'rate limit'.

question: map vs forEach vs reduce.
answer: map returns a new transformed array. forEach just iterates and returns nothing. reduce folds an array into a single value (sum, group-by, flatten, etc.).
tip: Use map when you need a result; forEach only for side effects.

question: Shallow copy vs deep copy.
answer:
Shallow copy ({...obj}, Object.assign) copies the top level only — nested objects remain shared references.

Deep copy clones everything: structuredClone(obj) (modern) or the old JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) (loses functions, dates, undefined).
tip: structuredClone is the modern one-liner for deep copies.
code:
const shallow = { ...original };
const deep = structuredClone(original);

question: Explain prototypal inheritance.
answer:
Every object has a hidden link ([[Prototype]]) to another object. When you access a property, JS walks up this prototype chain until it finds it or reaches null.

class syntax is sugar over this — methods live on the prototype so all instances share one copy.
tip: class ... extends is prototype-based inheritance under the hood.
code:
class Animal { speak() { return 'sound'; } }
class Dog extends Animal { speak() { return 'woof'; } }

question: What is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)?
answer: The span between entering a scope and the point where a let/const variable is declared. The binding is hoisted but not initialised, so accessing it in that window throws a ReferenceError.
tip: TDZ is why let/const 'feel' un-hoisted even though they technically are hoisted.
code:
console.log(x); // ReferenceError (TDZ)
let x = 5;
deep:
What it is

The TDZ is the region of a scope from its start up to the line that declares a let/const (or class). The binding exists (it was hoisted) but is uninitialised, so any read or write in that window throws a ReferenceError.

{
  // TDZ for x starts here
  console.log(x); // ❌ ReferenceError
  let x = 5;      // TDZ ends — x initialised
  console.log(x); // ✅ 5
}

Hoisted but not initialised

People say let/const "aren't hoisted" — technically they are, but unlike var (which is pre-set to undefined) they get no value until execution reaches the declaration. The TDZ is what enforces that.

Why it exists

It turns "using a variable before you declare it" into a loud error instead of a silent undefined — catching real bugs, and making const meaningful (a const can't be observed before it's assigned).

A subtle trap

let y = 1;
function f() {
  console.log(y); // ❌ ReferenceError, not 1
  let y = 2;      // this y shadows the outer one — whole fn is its TDZ
}

The inner let y shadows the outer, so the reference resolves to the inner binding, which is still in its TDZ.

typeof a;       // ❌ ReferenceError if a is a let in TDZ
typeof undeclared; // ✅ 'undefined' (never declared)

How to say it in an interview

“The Temporal Dead Zone is the gap between entering a scope and the let/const declaration line. The binding is hoisted but uninitialised, so touching it there throws a ReferenceError — that’s why let feels un-hoisted and why it catches use-before-declare bugs.”

question: Spread vs rest operator.
answer: Same ... syntax, opposite jobs. Spread expands an iterable into elements. Rest gathers remaining arguments/elements into an array.
tip: Spread 'spreads out'; rest 'collects the rest'.
code:
const merged = [...a, ...b];        // spread
function sum(...nums) {}            // rest
const { id, ...others } = obj;      // rest in destructuring

question: null vs undefined.
answer:
undefined — a variable declared but not assigned, or a missing property/return. Set by the engine.

null — an intentional 'no value', set by the developer.

Quirk: typeof null is 'object' (a historical bug).
tip: Use ?? (nullish coalescing) to default only on null/undefined, not on 0 or ''.
code: const name = input ?? 'Guest'; // keeps 0 and '' but replaces null/undefined

question: What is a higher-order function?
answer: A function that takes a function as an argument and/or returns a function. Examples: map, filter, setTimeout, and any function factory.
tip: Debounce, throttle, and curry are all higher-order functions.
code: const withLog = fn => (...args) => { console.log(args); return fn(...args); };

question: What is currying?
answer: Transforming a function that takes multiple arguments into a chain of functions that each take one argument. Enables partial application and reusable specialised functions.
tip: Currying leans on closures to remember earlier arguments.
code:
const add = a => b => c => a + b + c;
add(1)(2)(3); // 6

question: What is event delegation?
answer: Attach a single listener to a common parent and use event bubbling to handle events from many children (checked via event.target). Fewer listeners, and it works for dynamically added elements.
tip: Great answer for 'how would you handle clicks on a list of 1000 rows'.

question: Event bubbling vs capturing.
answer:
When an event fires, it travels capturing phase (top → target) then bubbling phase (target → top). Listeners run in the bubbling phase by default; pass { capture: true } for the capture phase.

stopPropagation() halts travel; preventDefault() cancels the default action.
tip: stopPropagation and preventDefault do different things — don't confuse them.
deep:
The three phases of event flow

Click a nested element and the event travels the DOM in order:

  1. Capturing — window → down toward the target.
  2. Target — reaches the element you interacted with.
  3. Bubbling — target → back up to window.

By default listeners fire in the bubbling phase. Pass { capture: true } (or true as the 3rd arg) to fire in the capturing phase instead.

el.addEventListener('click', fn);                 // bubbling
el.addEventListener('click', fn, { capture: true }); // capturing

Event delegation — the big payoff

Because events bubble, you can put one listener on a parent instead of many on children — great for lists and dynamically added items:

list.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
  const li = e.target.closest('li');
  if (li) select(li.dataset.id);
});

target vs currentTarget

  • e.target — the element that actually triggered the event (deepest).
  • e.currentTarget — the element whose listener is running (where you attached it).

stopPropagation vs preventDefault — not the same

MethodDoes
stopPropagation()stops the event travelling further (no more bubbling/capturing)
stopImmediatePropagation()that, plus skips other listeners on the same element
preventDefault()cancels the browser's default action (link nav, form submit) — travel continues

Non-bubbling events exist too — focus, blur, mouseenter (use focusin/mouseover if you need delegation).

How to say it in an interview

“An event captures down from the root to the target, then bubbles back up; listeners run on the bubbling phase unless I set capture: true. I lean on bubbling for event delegation — one listener on a parent. stopPropagation halts the travel, while preventDefault cancels the default action — different jobs.”

question: What is callback hell and how do you avoid it?
answer: Deeply nested callbacks (the 'pyramid of doom') that are hard to read and error-handle. Avoid it with Promises, async/await, and by extracting named functions.
tip: async/await is the modern cure for callback hell.

question: How do you make an object immutable?
answer: Object.freeze(obj) prevents adding, removing, or changing top-level properties (shallow). For deep immutability you must freeze nested objects recursively, or use a library.
tip: Immutability + OnPush is the pairing that makes Angular change detection fast.
code:
const cfg = Object.freeze({ mode: 'dark' });
cfg.mode = 'light'; // silently ignored (throws in strict mode)

question: When would you use a Set or a Map?
answer:
Set — a collection of unique values (dedupe an array with [...new Set(arr)]).

Map — key/value pairs where keys can be any type (objects included) and insertion order is preserved. Better than a plain object for frequent add/delete.
tip: Use a Set for fast has() lookups instead of array.includes in loops.

question: What do ?. and ?? do?
answer:
Optional chaining ?. short-circuits to undefined instead of throwing when accessing a property on null/undefined.

Nullish coalescing ?? returns the right side only when the left is null/undefined (unlike ||, it keeps 0, '', and false).
tip: Combine them: user?.profile?.name ?? 'Guest'.
code: const city = user?.address?.city ?? 'Unknown';

question: Function declaration vs expression vs arrow function.
answer:
• Declaration — hoisted fully, has its own this/arguments.
  • Expression — assigned to a variable, not hoisted (the variable is).
  • Arrow — concise, no own this/arguments/prototype; can't be a constructor.
tip: Never use an arrow as an object method that needs `this`, or as a constructor.
code:
function decl() {}         // hoisted
const expr = function () {};// not hoisted
const arrow = () => {};     // lexical this

question: What is an IIFE and the module pattern?
answer: An IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression) runs the moment it's defined, creating a private scope. The module pattern uses one to expose only a public API while keeping internals private via closures — the pre-ESM way to avoid globals.
tip: This app itself uses the IIFE pattern to register data without leaking globals.

question: Explain destructuring with defaults and renaming.
answer: Unpack arrays/objects into variables in one statement, with defaults for missing values and renaming for objects. Also enables clean swaps and nested extraction.
tip: Destructuring function params with defaults gives clean, self-documenting APIs.

question: Object.keys / values / entries / fromEntries.
answer: Object.keys/values/entries turn an object into arrays you can map/filter/reduce. Object.fromEntries rebuilds an object from key/value pairs — perfect for transforming objects.
tip: entries → map → fromEntries is the idiomatic 'map over an object' pattern.

question: JSON.stringify / parse — gotchas?
answer: Serialise/deserialise data. Gotchas: undefined, functions, and symbols are dropped; Date becomes a string; NaN/Infinity become null; circular references throw. A replacer/reviver function can customise both.
tip: JSON.stringify(obj, null, 2) pretty-prints — handy for debugging and exports.
code:
JSON.stringify({ a: 1 }, null, 2);
JSON.parse(str, (k, v) => k === 'date' ? new Date(v) : v);

question: How does fetch work, and how do you cancel a request?
answer: fetch returns a Promise of a Response. Note it only rejects on network failure — a 404/500 still resolves, so check res.ok. Cancel with an AbortController's signal.
tip: fetch doesn't throw on HTTP errors — always check res.ok yourself.

question: Promise.all vs allSettled vs race vs any.
answer:
• all — resolves with all results, rejects on the first failure.
  • allSettled — waits for all, returns status for each (never rejects).
  • race — settles as soon as the first one settles (resolve or reject).
  • any — resolves with the first success, rejects only if all fail.
tip: Use allSettled when you want every result even if some calls fail.
code:
await Promise.allSettled([a(), b(), c()]);
// [{status:'fulfilled',value},{status:'rejected',reason}, ...]

question: What are generators and iterators?
answer: A generator (function*) can pause with yield and resume, producing values lazily. It returns an iterator (has .next()). Great for infinite/lazy sequences and custom iteration.
tip: Any object with a [Symbol.iterator] method works in for...of and spread.

question: What is a Symbol and where is it used?
answer: A Symbol is a unique, immutable primitive often used as a non-colliding object key. Well-known symbols like Symbol.iterator let you hook into language behaviour (making an object iterable).
tip: Symbols create 'hidden' keys that won't clash with string keys or show in for...in.
code:
const id = Symbol('id');
obj[id] = 123; // unique, non-enumerable-ish key

question: WeakMap / WeakSet — when and why?
answer: They hold object keys weakly — if nothing else references the key, it can be garbage-collected, preventing leaks. Keys must be objects; they're not iterable. Use them for private per-object metadata or caches tied to an object's lifetime.
tip: A WeakMap cache won't keep DOM nodes alive after they're removed — no leak.
code:
const cache = new WeakMap();
cache.set(domNode, computed); // freed when domNode is

question: Why does a for-loop with var log the wrong value in setTimeout?
answer: var is function-scoped, so every callback shares the same variable, which by the time the timers fire equals the final value. let is block-scoped — each iteration gets a fresh binding, so it logs correctly.
tip: The classic fix is just changing var to let — each iteration captures its own i.
code:
for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) setTimeout(() => console.log(i));
// 0, 1, 2   (with var → 3, 3, 3)

question: typeof vs instanceof.
answer: typeof returns a string for primitives ('string', 'number', 'function'…). instanceof checks the prototype chain for objects/classes. Note typeof null === 'object' and arrays are 'object' — use Array.isArray.
tip: Use Array.isArray(x), not typeof, to detect arrays.
code:
typeof []; // 'object'
Array.isArray([]); // true
[] instanceof Array; // true

question: How do you check for NaN correctly?
answer: NaN is the only value not equal to itself, so NaN === NaN is false. Use Number.isNaN(x) (strict — no coercion) rather than the global isNaN(), which coerces (isNaN('foo') is true).
tip: Prefer Number.isNaN over the global isNaN to avoid coercion surprises.
code:
Number.isNaN(NaN);    // true
Number.isNaN('foo');  // false
isNaN('foo');         // true (coerced!)

question: How do you update the DOM efficiently?
answer: Minimise reflows/repaints: batch DOM writes, build nodes in a DocumentFragment and append once, avoid reading layout (offsetWidth) between writes (layout thrashing), and prefer class toggles over inline style loops. Use requestAnimationFrame for visual updates.
tip: Appending 100 nodes one-by-one triggers 100 reflows; a DocumentFragment triggers one.

question: What is a pure function and why prefer it?
answer: A pure function always returns the same output for the same input and has no side effects (no mutation, I/O, or external state). Pure functions are predictable, testable, cacheable, and safe to parallelise — the basis of reducers and OnPush change detection.
tip: Reducers in Redux/NgRx must be pure — that's what enables time-travel debugging.
code:
const add = (a, b) => a + b;            // pure
let total = 0; const addTo = n => total += n; // impure

question: What are the data types in JavaScript?
answer:
Primitive (immutable, copied by value): string, number, boolean, bigint, symbol, undefined, null.

Non-primitive (reference type): object — which includes arrays, functions, and dates.
tip: Primitives are compared/copied by value; objects by reference — a very common gotcha.
code:
typeof 'a';     // 'string'
typeof 10n;     // 'bigint'
typeof null;    // 'object' (historical bug)
typeof [];      // 'object'

question: How do you convert between strings and numbers?
answer:
Number → string: String(n), n.toString(), or a template literal `${n}`.

String → number: Number(s), parseInt(s) (integer), parseFloat(s) (decimal), or the unary +s.
tip: parseInt('123px') = 123 (stops at non-digits); Number('123px') = NaN (strict).

question: Synchronous vs asynchronous programming.
answer: Synchronous code runs line by line — each task blocks the next until it finishes. Asynchronous code lets long-running work (network, timers, I/O) happen in the background and continue later via callbacks/promises/async-await, keeping the single UI thread responsive.
tip: JS is single-threaded, so async is how it stays responsive during I/O.

question: Is JavaScript single-threaded or multi-threaded?
answer: JavaScript is single-threaded — one call stack, one thing at a time. It feels concurrent because of the event loop: async work is offloaded to the browser/Node APIs and their callbacks are queued back onto the stack when it's free. (Web Workers add real parallel threads, but they don't share the main thread.)
tip: One thread + event loop = the mental model for all async behaviour in JS.
deep:
Single-threaded, but concurrent

JavaScript has one call stack — it executes one statement at a time and can't run two pieces of JS in parallel on the main thread. What makes it feel concurrent is that slow work (timers, network, file I/O) is handed off to the host (browser or Node), which runs it elsewhere and queues a callback back when it's done.

Who does the async work?

The engine (V8) is single-threaded, but the runtime around it isn't. The browser provides Web APIs and a thread pool; Node uses libuv with its own worker threads. Your JS callback still runs back on the one main thread via the event loop.

console.log('start');
setTimeout(() => console.log('later'), 0); // offloaded to timer API
console.log('end');
// start, end, later

Why single-threaded is a deliberate choice

No shared-memory threads on the main thread means no data races or locks in your app code — simpler and safe for DOM manipulation. The cost: a long synchronous loop blocks everything (UI freezes), so you must keep the main thread free.

Getting real parallelism

  • Web Workers — separate threads for CPU-heavy work; communicate by messages, no shared DOM.
  • Node worker_threads — the Node equivalent.
  • SharedArrayBuffer / Atomics — actual shared memory between workers (advanced).

How to say it in an interview

“JavaScript itself is single-threaded — one call stack. It handles async work by offloading it to the browser or Node runtime and queuing the callbacks, which the event loop runs back on that single thread. For true parallelism I’d use Web Workers, which run on separate threads and communicate via messages.”

question: What is an event listener?
answer: A function registered to run when a specific event (click, input, mouseover, …) fires on a DOM element, via addEventListener. Remove it with removeEventListener to avoid leaks.
tip: Use addEventListener (not onclick=) so you can attach multiple handlers and remove them.
code:
const btn = document.getElementById('save');
btn.addEventListener('click', () => console.log('clicked'));

question: slice() vs splice().
answer: slice(start, end) returns a copy of a portion — it does not mutate the original. splice(start, count, …items) mutates the array, removing and/or inserting elements, and returns the removed ones.
tip: slice = safe copy; splice = in-place edit. Easy to mix up — remember 'p' for 'mutate in place'.

question: call() vs apply() vs bind().
answer: All set this explicitly. call invokes immediately with arguments listed individually; apply invokes immediately with arguments as an array; bind returns a new function with this permanently bound (call it later).
tip: call/apply invoke now; bind returns a function to invoke later. apply takes an Array.
deep:
All three set this — they differ in when and how args are passed

MethodInvokesArguments
callimmediatelylisted individually
applyimmediatelyas one array
bindlater (returns a new fn)listed; can pre-fill some
function greet(g, p) { return `${g}, ${this.name}${p}`; }
const user = { name: 'Asha' };

greet.call(user, 'Hi', '!');       // 'Hi, Asha!'
greet.apply(user, ['Hi', '!']);    // 'Hi, Asha!'
const g = greet.bind(user);        // new function
g('Hi', '!');                      // 'Hi, Asha!'

Mnemonic: Apply = Array. Call = comma-separated. Bind = build-for-later.

bind also does partial application

function multiply(a, b) { return a * b; }
const double = multiply.bind(null, 2);
double(5); // 10  — first arg pre-filled

Real uses

  • Fix a lost this when passing a method as a callback: btn.addEventListener('click', this.handle.bind(this)).
  • Borrow methods: Array.prototype.slice.call(arguments) to arrayify array-likes.
  • Spread into a variadic fn: Math.max.apply(null, nums) (modern: Math.max(...nums)).

Caveats

You can't rebind an arrow function's this (it has none). And bind is permanent — binding an already-bound function again has no effect.

How to say it in an interview

“All three explicitly set this. call and apply invoke immediately — call takes comma-separated args, apply takes an array. bind returns a new function with this permanently fixed to call later, and can pre-fill arguments for partial application.”

question: What are template literals?
answer: Strings written in backticks that support interpolation (${expr}) and multi-line text without concatenation. Tagged template literals let a function process the parts (used for i18n, styled-components, safe HTML).
tip: Backticks + ${} replace clunky string concatenation and allow multi-line strings.
code:
const name = 'Chi';
const msg = `Hello ${name}, ${1 + 1} new alerts`;

question: How does garbage collection work in JavaScript?
answer: The engine automatically frees memory for objects that are no longer reachable from a root (global, current call stack). The main algorithm is mark-and-sweep. Leaks still happen via lingering references — forgotten timers, detached DOM nodes, or global variables.
tip: You can't force GC; you prevent leaks by clearing references, timers, and listeners.

question: How do you add or access properties dynamically on an object?
answer: Use bracket notation when the key is dynamic or not a valid identifier: obj[key] = value. Dot notation (obj.key) only works for fixed, valid names.
tip: Bracket notation is how you set a property whose name is in a variable.

question: for...of vs for...in.
answer: for…of iterates the values of an iterable (arrays, strings, Maps, Sets). for…in iterates the keys (property names) of an object — and can pick up inherited enumerable keys, so it's not ideal for arrays.
tip: for…of for arrays/values, for…in for object keys. Don't loop arrays with for…in.
code:
for (const v of ['a', 'b']) {}        // 'a', 'b'
for (const k in { x: 1, y: 2 }) {}     // 'x', 'y'

question: setTimeout() vs setInterval().
answer: setTimeout runs a callback once after a delay. setInterval runs it repeatedly at a fixed interval. Both return an id you clear with clearTimeout/clearInterval.
tip: Always clear intervals (e.g. in cleanup) or they run forever and leak.
code:
const t = setTimeout(fn, 1000);   clearTimeout(t);
const i = setInterval(fn, 1000);  clearInterval(i);

question: What is variable shadowing?
answer: When a variable declared in an inner scope has the same name as one in an outer scope, the inner one shadows (hides) the outer within that block. Common with function parameters and block-scoped let/const.
tip: Illegal shadowing: you can't shadow a let with a var in the same scope.
deep:
What it is

Shadowing happens when a variable in an inner scope has the same name as one in an enclosing scope. Inside the inner scope, the name resolves to the inner variable — the outer one is hidden (not overwritten).

let x = 1;
function f() {
  let x = 2;   // shadows the outer x
  return x;    // 2
}
f();           // 2
x;             // 1 — outer untouched

It follows the scope chain

JS resolves a name by walking outward: current block → enclosing function → … → global. The nearest declaration wins, so the inner one shadows the rest.

const v = 'global';
{
  const v = 'block';
  console.log(v); // 'block'
}
console.log(v);   // 'global'

Illegal shadowing

You can shadow across nested scopes, but you can't create a conflicting redeclaration in the same scope — mixing let and var for the same name throws:

function bad() {
  let a = 1;
  { var a = 2; } // ❌ SyntaxError: 'a' already declared
}

The loop-variable case

for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) { /* each iteration: a fresh i */ }

let in a loop head effectively creates a new binding per iteration — a form of scoping that shadows across iterations and fixes the classic closure-in-loop bug.

Should you do it?

Deliberate shadowing (e.g. narrowing a param inside a block) is fine and common. Accidental shadowing hides bugs — a linter rule (no-shadow) catches unintended cases.

How to say it in an interview

“Shadowing is when an inner-scope variable reuses an outer variable’s name and hides it within that scope — name resolution picks the nearest one. The outer value is untouched. It’s legal across nested scopes but not as a conflicting redeclaration in the same scope.”

question: Generator function vs normal function.
answer: A normal function runs to completion and returns once. A generator (function*) can pause at each yield and resume later, returning an iterator. It's lazy — values are produced on demand, enabling infinite sequences and custom iteration.
tip: Generators return an iterator you drive with .next(); normal functions just return a value.

question: Mutable vs immutable in JavaScript.
answer: Primitives (string, number, boolean…) are immutable — operations produce new values, never change the original. Objects and arrays are mutable — their contents can change in place. Freeze an object with Object.freeze to prevent top-level mutation.
tip: String methods return NEW strings — the original never changes.
code:
let s = 'abc'; s.toUpperCase(); // 'ABC' but s is still 'abc'
const a = [1]; a.push(2);       // a mutated to [1, 2]

question: What are truthy and falsy values?
answer: In a boolean context (if, &&, !), every value coerces to true or false. There are exactly 8 falsy values: false, 0, -0, 0n, "", null, undefined, and NaN. Everything else is truthy — including "0", "false", [], and {}.
tip: Empty array [] and empty object {} are TRUTHY — the classic gotcha.
code:
if ([]) console.log('runs');   // [] is truthy!
Boolean('0');  // true
Boolean(0);    // false
Boolean(NaN);  // false
deep:
The 8 falsy values (memorise these)

false
0    -0    0n        // zero, incl. BigInt
""                  // empty string
null
undefined
NaN

Anything not in that list is truthy. The surprising ones: "0", " ", "false", [], {}, and any function are all truthy.

Where it bites

if (arr.length) { }        // ✅ 0 is falsy, so empty array skips
if (arr) { }               // ❌ [] is truthy, always runs

if (count) { }             // ❌ skips when count === 0 (valid!)
if (count != null) { }     // ✅ only skips null/undefined

Short-circuiting uses this

const name = input || 'Guest';   // '' is falsy → 'Guest'
const n = value ?? 0;            // ?? only catches null/undefined, keeps 0/''

Prefer ?? over || when 0 or "" are valid values you don't want replaced.

How to say it in an interview

“There are exactly eight falsy values — false, 0, -0, 0n, "", null, undefined, NaN — and everything else is truthy, including empty arrays and objects. So I check arr.length, not the array itself.”

question: What does "use strict" do?
answer:
Strict mode ('use strict'; at the top of a file or function) opts into a stricter JS variant that catches common mistakes as errors: it forbids undeclared globals, makes silent failures throw, disallows duplicate parameter names, and sets this to undefined in plain function calls (instead of the global object).

ES modules and class bodies are always strict, so you rarely write it by hand anymore.
tip: Inside a module or a class, you're already in strict mode — no directive needed.
code:
'use strict';
x = 10; // ReferenceError: x is not defined (no accidental global)

question: find, filter, some, every, includes — what do they do?
answer:
• filter — returns a new array of all items passing a test.
  • find — returns the first matching item (or undefined).
  • findIndex — the index of the first match (or -1).
  • some — true if at least one item passes.
  • every — true if all items pass.
  • includes — true if the array contains a value (uses ===).
tip: find returns the item; filter returns an array. Use find when you expect one result.
code:
const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4];
nums.filter(n => n % 2 === 0); // [2, 4]
nums.find(n => n > 2);        // 3
nums.some(n => n > 3);        // true
nums.every(n => n > 0);       // true
nums.includes(2);             // true
